nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; juveniles tend to shelter in mangroves, sheltered coastal reefs, caves, and under ledges. Adults are generally solitary under reef overhangs at depths of about 15–30 m.

decorPreferences: tall aquascape (vertical clearance for tall-bodied fish)

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented eating algae, jellyfish, and gelatinous zooplankton, plus small invertebrates. In captivity is notoriously difficult to feed and frequently refuses prepared foods, particularly as a juvenile. A varied diet of meaty foods (mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, chopped shrimp/scallops/mussels) alongside marine algae and seaweed is generally advised, offered in small amounts multiple times daily. Live foods (live brine shrimp, small live shrimp) are commonly required to initiate feeding in newly imported wild specimens; tank-raised juveniles tend to adapt more readily to prepared foods.

summary: The Pinnatus Batfish (Platax pinnatus) is a striking tall-bodied Western Pacific reef fish whose jet-black juvenile body is rimmed with brilliant crimson margins, fading to a duller silver-brown as the fish matures. Generally regarded as an expert-only species due to its notorious refusal to feed in captivity and high stress sensitivity.
careNotes:
The Pinnatus Batfish is one of the most challenging marine fish commonly offered in the aquarium trade. The species is widely flagged as expert-only across hobby sources, primarily because newly imported wild-caught specimens frequently refuse to eat and waste away within weeks. Tank-raised juveniles, when available, tend to fare significantly better.

Tank size and footprint: Sources generally recommend an absolute minimum of 757–1136 L (200–300 US gal), with ReefApp citing 1200 L. A 1000 L+ tank is generally advised, biased toward the larger end of the range due to the species' tall, disc-shaped body, fast growth, and need for ample unobstructed vertical and horizontal swimming space. Depth (front-to-back) and height matter as much as length; very tall fish are commonly cramped in standard-profile reef tanks.

Juvenile/adult color phase: Juveniles display a dramatic jet-black body with brilliant crimson/red margins outlining the entire body, fins, and tail — a coloration thought to mimic toxic flatworms (Pseudoceros). The red margins fade with maturity, and adults transition to a dull silver-brown to silver-tan with vertical banding and shorter fins. The visual change is extreme enough that juveniles and adults are sometimes mistaken for different species.

Feeding: The most cited reason for keeper failure. Many wild-caught individuals never adapt to prepared foods. Live brine shrimp, small live ghost shrimp, and live black worms are commonly used to initiate feeding; gradual weaning onto frozen mysis, enriched brine, and chopped seafood is the typical path. Macroalgae and seaweed strips on a clip have been reported to be accepted by acclimated individuals. Feeding multiple small meals per day is generally advised.

Reef compatibility: Not reef safe. Has been documented grazing on coral polyps, anemones, and small ornamental invertebrates. A FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) configuration is generally recommended.

Health and shipping: Highly susceptible to marine ich (Cryptocaryon) and marine velvet (Amyloodinium), particularly during and shortly after shipping. Long quarantine periods and slow acclimation are generally advised. Shipping mortality is reported to be high, which contributes to the species' reputation for poor captive survival.

Cross-source disagreements (within tolerance, no needs_review): Maximum adult size varied — LiveAquaria states up to ~50 cm (20 in), FishBase and ReefApp 45 cm, Fishkeeping World cites 16–30 in (40–76 cm, likely an outlier). Final 35–45 cm range reflects the consensus of FishBase and ReefApp. Temperature ranges varied from 22.2–25.6 °C (LiveAquaria) to 25–29 °C (FishBase wild data); averaged to 24–27 °C for aquarium use. Tank size cited 200–300 US gal (757–1136 L) by retailers and 1200 L by ReefApp; rounded to 1000 L as the conservative aquarist-friendly value.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the home-aquarium hobby. Like other Ephippidae, spawning in the wild is believed to be pelagic broadcast spawning, with larvae developing in open water before juveniles settle into mangroves and sheltered coastal reefs.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: 200–300 gal tank, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, expert-only, peaceful but not reef safe, juveniles have red border that fades, adults reach ~20 in, omnivore requiring varied meaty foods three times daily.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Ephippidae, order Acanthuriformes — note LiveAquaria's 'Ephippididae' spelling is incorrect), max length 45 cm TL, depth range 15–30 m, temperature 25–29 °C, native range Western Pacific Ryukyu Islands to Australia (Indian Ocean occurrence doubtful), IUCN Least Concern, diet on algae/jellyfish/gelatinous zooplankton, adults solitary under reef overhangs, juveniles in mangroves.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum 200 gal (ideally 300+), 77–80°F, pH 8.2–8.4, SG 1.018–1.025, lifespan 5–12 years (up to 14 recorded), peaceful temperament, omnivore, juvenile color phase, highly susceptible to ich and marine velvet, good tankmates list, fast growth.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 45 cm, recommended minimum tank 1200 L, ~26 °C, SG ~1.026, mostly peaceful but possible aggression toward conspecifics, not reef safe (threatens anemones, shrimps, crabs, corals), delicate, susceptible to marine ich when stressed.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding in the home aquarium hobby; pelagic broadcast spawner with larval development in open water.
